title: Count HTTP Status Codes in Access Log
prompt: Write a Python script `status_count.py` that reads an Apache-style access log file passed as a command-line argument and outputs a summary of HTTP status codes. The output must be written to `status_report.txt` with one line per status code in the format `STATUS_CODE: COUNT`, sorted by status code ascending. Include a final line `Total: N` where N is the total number of requests.

title: Extract Top IPs from Access Log
prompt: Write a Python script `top_ips.py` that reads an Apache access log file (first argument) and writes the top 3 IP addresses by request count to `top_ips.txt`. Each line should be formatted as `IP ADDRESS  REQUESTS` (two spaces between), sorted by count descending. If there is a tie, sort alphabetically by IP.

title: Parse Disk Usage Report
prompt: Write a Python script `disk_report.py` that reads a `du_output.txt` file (passed as first argument) containing lines in the format `SIZE_KB\tPATH` and produces `disk_report.txt`. The report must list all entries larger than 1000 KB sorted by size descending, with each line formatted as `PATH: SIZE_KB KB`. Include a final line `Total large entries: N`.

title: Validate SSH Config Syntax
prompt: Write a Python script `ssh_check.py` that reads an SSH-style config file (first argument) and validates that every `Host` block has both a `HostName` and a `User` field. Write `ssh_validation.txt` with one line per Host block: `Host NAME: OK` or `Host NAME: MISSING HostName|User`. The last line should be `Valid: N/M` where N is valid count and M is total count.

input.ssh_config:
Host webserver
  HostName 192.168.1.100
  User deploy
  Port 22

Host database
  HostName 10.0.0.50
  Port 5432

Host staging
  HostName 172.16.0.10
  User admin
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/staging_key

Host mystery
  User ghost

title: Parse Process List for Resource Hogs
prompt: Write a Python script `proc_check.py` that reads a file (first argument) resembling `ps aux` output and identifies processes using more than 5.0% CPU or more than 10.0% memory. Write results to `alerts.txt` with one line per offending process: `ALERT: PID USER CPU% MEM% COMMAND`. End with a summary line `Alerts: N`.

input.ps_output.txt:
USER       PID %CPU %MEM    VSZ   RSS TTY      STAT START   TIME COMMAND
root         1  0.0  0.1  16548  1024 ?        Ss   08:00   0:01 /sbin/init
www-data  1234  8.5  3.2 125000 32768 ?        S    08:01   2:15 /usr/sbin/apache2
postgres  2345  2.1 15.4 500000 157696 ?       S    08:02   5:30 postgres: writer process
mysql     3456  6.2  8.8 300000 90112 ?        S    08:03   3:45 /usr/sbin/mysqld
nobody    4567  0.1  0.2   5000  2048 ?        S    08:04   0:00 /usr/bin/dbus-daemon
root      5678 12.3 11.5 800000 117760 ?       R    08:05   8:20 /opt/app/worker
user      6789  0.3  0.5  12000  5120 pts/0    S+   08:06   0:02 vim config.txt

title: Parse Crontab and List Schedules
prompt: Write a Python script `cron_list.py` that reads a crontab file (first argument), ignores comments and blank lines, and writes a human-readable schedule to `cron_report.txt`. Each line should be `COMMAND — runs MINUTE HOUR DOM MONTH DOW`. End with `Total jobs: N`.

title: Simple Uptime SLA Calculator
prompt: Write a Python script `sla_calc.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing an array of objects with `timestamp` and `status` fields (status is `up` or `down`). Calculate the uptime percentage as `(up_count / total_count) * 100` rounded to 2 decimal places. Write `sla_report.txt` containing: `Total checks: N`, `Up: N`, `Down: N`, `Uptime: XX.XX%`.

title: Detect Duplicate User UIDs
prompt: Write a Python script `uid_audit.py` that reads a passwd-style file (first argument) and detects any duplicate UIDs. Each line in the passwd file has the format `username:x:UID:GID:info:home:shell`. Write `uid_report.txt` listing each duplicate UID group as `UID UID_NUM: user1, user2, ...` (one line per duplicate UID). End with `Duplicates found: N` (count of duplicate UID groups, 0 if none).

input.passwd:
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
daemon:x:1:1:daemon:/usr/sbin:/usr/sbin/nologin
www-data:x:33:33:www-data:/var/www:/usr/sbin/nologin
nginx:x:33:33:nginx:/var/www:/usr/sbin/nologin
postgres:x:108:114:PostgreSQL:/var/lib/postgresql:/bin/bash
mysql:x:109:115:MySQL Server:/var/lib/mysql:/bin/false
backup_user:x:0:0:Backup Root:/backup:/bin/bash
nobody:x:65534:65534:nobody:/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin
appuser:x:1001:1001:App User:/home/appuser:/bin/bash
deployuser:x:1001:1001:Deploy:/home/deploy:/bin/bash

title: Generate Backup Manifest
prompt: Write a Python script `backup_manifest.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing an array of objects with fields `filename`, `size_bytes`, and `md5`. Generate `manifest.txt` with each line as `MD5  SIZE  FILENAME` (separated by two spaces). Include a header line `# Backup Manifest — FILE_COUNT files, TOTAL_SIZE bytes`. The total size should be the sum of all size_bytes values.

title: Extract Error Lines from Syslog
prompt: Write a Python script `syslog_errors.py` that reads a syslog-style file (first argument) and extracts all lines containing the words `error`, `critical`, or `fatal` (case-insensitive). Write matching lines to `errors.txt` preserving original order. Write a summary to `error_summary.txt` with counts: `error: N`, `critical: N`, `fatal: N`, `total: N`.

input.syslog:
Apr 10 08:00:01 server1 systemd[1]: Started Session 42.
Apr 10 08:00:05 server1 kernel: EXT4-fs error (device sda1): bad inode
Apr 10 08:01:12 server1 sshd[2345]: Accepted publickey for admin
Apr 10 08:02:30 server1 nginx[789]: 2026/04/10 08:02:30 [error] upstream timed out
Apr 10 08:03:45 server1 kernel: CRITICAL: thermal zone 0 exceeded threshold
Apr 10 08:04:00 server1 cron[101]: (root) CMD (/usr/local/bin/check.sh)

Apr 10 08:08:00 server1 sshd[2346]: Failed password for invalid user test

title: Check SSL Certificate Expiry Dates
prompt: Write a Python script `cert_check.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) with an array of objects containing `domain`, `issuer`, and `expiry_date` (YYYY-MM-DD format). The script should check each certificate against the date 2026-04-10. Write `cert_report.txt` with one line per domain: `domain — VALID (N days remaining)` or `domain — EXPIRED (N days ago)`. End with `Expired: N, Valid: M`.

title: Parse Ping Output for Latency Stats
prompt: Write a Python script `ping_stats.py` that reads a file (first argument) containing ping-like output lines in the format `64 bytes from HOST: icmp_seq=N ttl=T time=X.Y ms`. Extract all time values and write `ping_report.txt` with: `Packets: N`, `Min: X.XX ms`, `Max: X.XX ms`, `Avg: X.XX ms`, `Packet loss: 0%` (assume all packets arrived).

PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=1 ttl=117 time=12.3 ms

64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=6 ttl=117 time=11.0 ms
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=7 ttl=117 time=14.7 ms
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=8 ttl=117 time=12.0 ms

rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 10.5/16.325/45.2/10.873 ms

title: Server Inventory Summary from CSV
prompt: Write a Python script `inventory.py` that reads a CSV file (first argument) with columns `hostname,ip,os,role,status` and generates `inventory_report.txt`. The report should contain: count of servers per OS, count per role, count per status, and total server count. Format each section with a header like `--- By OS ---` followed by `OS_NAME: COUNT` lines sorted alphabetically.

hostname,ip,os,role,status

backup01,192.168.1.60,Debian 12,backup,active
staging01,192.168.1.70,CentOS 9,web,inactive
lb01,192.168.1.80,Ubuntu 22.04,loadbalancer,active

title: Identify Failed SSH Logins
prompt: Write a Python script `ssh_fails.py` that reads an auth-log-style file (first argument) and extracts all lines containing `Failed password`. Count failures per source IP. Write `ssh_fail_report.txt` with lines formatted as `IP: COUNT failures` sorted by count descending. End with `Total failed attempts: N`.

title: Nginx Config Generator from JSON
prompt: Write a Python script `nginx_gen.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing an array of site definitions, each with `domain`, `port`, `root`, and optional `ssl` (boolean). Generate a valid nginx config file `sites.conf` with one `server` block per site. SSL sites should listen on 443 with `ssl_certificate` and `ssl_certificate_key` paths at `/etc/ssl/DOMAIN.crt` and `/etc/ssl/DOMAIN.key`. Non-SSL sites listen on 80. Each block must include `server_name`, `root`, and a `location /` with `proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:PORT`.

title: Log Rotation Simulator
prompt: Write a Python script `logrotate.py` that simulates log rotation. It takes a log file path (first argument) and a max size in bytes (second argument). If the file exceeds the max size, it should: (1) rename the existing file by appending `.1` (if `.1` exists, rename it to `.2`, etc., up to `.3` — delete `.3` if it exists before shifting), (2) create a new empty file with the original name. Write a summary to `rotation_log.txt` listing actions taken. If the file does not exceed the max size, write `No rotation needed — FILE_SIZE bytes < MAX_SIZE bytes`.

input.app.log:
2026-04-10 08:00:01 INFO Application started successfully
2026-04-10 08:00:02 DEBUG Loading configuration from /etc/app/config.yml
2026-04-10 08:00:03 INFO Connected to database at 10.0.0.50:5432
2026-04-10 08:00:04 WARN Connection pool nearing capacity: 45/50
2026-04-10 08:00:05 ERROR Failed to process request ID 12345: timeout after 30s

title: CPU and Memory Trend Analysis
prompt: Write a Python script `resource_trend.py` that reads a CSV file (first argument) with columns `timestamp,cpu_percent,memory_percent,disk_percent`. Compute: average, min, max for each metric. Detect any metric that exceeds 90% and list those timestamps as alerts. Write `resource_report.txt` with sections `--- CPU ---`, `--- Memory ---`, `--- Disk ---` each with Avg/Min/Max lines, then a `--- Alerts ---` section listing `TIMESTAMP: METRIC at VALUE%`. End with `Alert count: N`.

title: Health Check Script with Port Simulation
prompt: Write a Python script `health_check.py` that reads a JSON config file (first argument) defining services to check, each with `name`, `host`, `port`, and `expected_status`. Since we cannot do real network checks, the script should read a second JSON file `service_state.json` (second argument) that maps `host:port` to `up` or `down`. Compare actual state to expected. Write `health_report.txt` with one line per service: `[OK] name (host:port)` or `[FAIL] name (host:port) — expected STATUS, got ACTUAL`. End with `Healthy: N/M`.

title: Backup Rotation Policy Enforcer
prompt: Write a Python script `backup_rotate.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing an array of backup records with `filename`, `date` (YYYY-MM-DD), and `size_bytes`. Apply a retention policy: keep all backups from the last 7 days (relative to 2026-04-10), keep only one per week for 8-30 days old (the newest in each week), delete anything older than 30 days. Write `retention_plan.txt` listing each backup as `KEEP filename (reason)` or `DELETE filename (reason)`. End with `Keep: N, Delete: M, Space saved: S bytes`.

title: Alert Rule Evaluator Against Metrics
prompt: Write a Python script `alert_eval.py` that reads two JSON files: a rules file (first argument) with an array of objects containing `name`, `metric`, `operator` (gt, lt, gte, lte, eq), `threshold`, and `severity`; and a metrics file (second argument) with an object mapping metric names to current values. Evaluate each rule against the metrics. Write `alert_output.json` as a JSON array of triggered alerts with `rule_name`, `metric`, `current_value`, `threshold`, `severity`. Also write `alert_summary.txt` with `Rules evaluated: N`, `Alerts triggered: M`, and one line per triggered alert: `[SEVERITY] rule_name: metric = value (threshold: OP threshold)`.

title: Parse Traceroute and Detect Latency Spikes
prompt: Write a Python script `traceroute_analyze.py` that reads a traceroute-style output file (first argument). Each hop line has format `N  HOSTNAME (IP)  TIME1 ms  TIME2 ms  TIME3 ms` (some may show `* * *` for timeouts). Compute average latency per hop, identify hops where average latency exceeds the previous hop by more than 50ms (latency spike), and detect timeout hops. Write `traceroute_report.txt` with each hop as `Hop N: HOST (IP) avg=X.XX ms` or `Hop N: * * * (timeout)`, a `--- Spikes ---` section listing spike hops, and `--- Summary ---` with total hops, timeout count, and spike count.

title: Multi-Service Log Aggregator
prompt: Write a Python script `log_aggregate.py` that reads multiple log files specified as command-line arguments. Each log line starts with a timestamp in ISO format `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS`. The script must: (1) merge all lines chronologically, (2) tag each line with its source filename, (3) write the merged output to `merged.log` with format `[SOURCE] ORIGINAL_LINE`. Also write `aggregate_stats.txt` with per-file line counts and the total across all files.

input.app.log:
2026-04-10T08:00:02 INFO Starting request handler
2026-04-10T08:00:06 DEBUG Processing API call
2026-04-10T08:00:13 INFO Form submission received
2026-04-10T08:00:15 WARN Slow query detected: 250ms
input.db.log:
2026-04-10T08:00:03 QUERY SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10 — 5ms
2026-04-10T08:00:07 QUERY SELECT * FROM data WHERE id=42 — 12ms
2026-04-10T08:00:14 INSERT INTO submissions VALUES(...) — 8ms

title: Firewall Rule Conflict Detector
prompt: Write a Python script `fw_check.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing firewall rules, each with `id`, `action` (allow/deny), `protocol` (tcp/udp/any), `src_ip` (CIDR or `any`), `dst_port` (integer or `any`), and `priority` (lower = higher priority). Detect conflicts: two rules that match the same traffic (overlapping src_ip and dst_port and protocol) but have different actions. Write `fw_report.txt` listing each conflict pair as `CONFLICT: rule ID1 (ACTION) vs rule ID2 (ACTION) — REASON`. End with `Total conflicts: N`. For this task, treat `any` as matching everything, and two IPs conflict if they are identical or either is `any`.

title: DNS Zone File Validator
prompt: Write a Python script `dns_validate.py` that reads a simplified DNS zone file (first argument). Valid record types are A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS. Each line has format `NAME TTL TYPE VALUE` (space-separated, TTL is an integer). Validate: (1) TTL must be between 60 and 86400, (2) A records must have valid IPv4 addresses, (3) CNAME records cannot coexist with other records for the same NAME, (4) MX records must have format `PRIORITY DOMAIN`. Write `dns_report.txt` with `[VALID] line` or `[ERROR] line — reason` for each record, and a summary `Valid: N, Errors: M`.

title: Apache VHost Analyzer
prompt: Write a Python script `vhost_analyze.py` that reads an Apache-style VirtualHost configuration file (first argument) and extracts information about each VirtualHost block. For each block, capture the `ServerName`, `DocumentRoot`, `Port` (from the `<VirtualHost *:PORT>` line), and whether SSL is enabled (look for `SSLEngine on`). Write `vhost_report.json` as a JSON array of objects with fields `server_name`, `document_root`, `port`, `ssl_enabled`. Also write `vhost_summary.txt` with total VHosts count, SSL-enabled count, and a list of all server names.

input.vhosts.conf:
<VirtualHost *:80>
    ServerName example.com
    DocumentRoot /var/www/example
    ErrorLog ${APACHE_LOG_DIR}/example-error.log
</VirtualHost>

<VirtualHost *:443>
    ServerName secure.example.com
    DocumentRoot /var/www/secure
    SSLEngine on
    SSLCertificateFile /etc/ssl/secure.crt
    SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/ssl/secure.key
</VirtualHost>

<VirtualHost *:80>
    ServerName blog.example.com
    DocumentRoot /var/www/blog
    ErrorLog ${APACHE_LOG_DIR}/blog-error.log
</VirtualHost>

<VirtualHost *:443>
    ServerName api.example.com
    DocumentRoot /var/www/api
    SSLEngine on
    SSLCertificateFile /etc/ssl/api.crt
    SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/ssl/api.key
</VirtualHost>

<VirtualHost *:8080>
    ServerName internal.example.com
    DocumentRoot /var/www/internal
</VirtualHost>

title: Cron Schedule Conflict Finder
prompt: Write a Python script `cron_conflicts.py` that reads a crontab file (first argument) and detects jobs that would run at the same time. Parse standard 5-field cron expressions (minute hour dom month dow). Two jobs conflict if they share any common execution time within a 24-hour window (use 2026-04-10 as the reference day). Write `cron_conflicts.txt` with each conflict pair on a line: `CONFLICT: "CMD1" and "CMD2" both run at HH:MM`. End with `Total conflicts: N`.

title: Permission Audit from File Listing
prompt: Write a Python script `perm_audit.py` that reads a file (first argument) containing `ls -la` style output. Detect security issues: (1) world-writable files (permissions ending in `w`), (2) SUID/SGID binaries (permissions with `s`), (3) files owned by root that are group/world writable. Write `perm_audit.txt` with each finding as `[RISK_LEVEL] path — reason` where RISK_LEVEL is HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW. End with counts per risk level and total findings.

drwxr-xr-x  5 root  root   4096 Apr 10 08:00 .

drwxrwxrwt  2 root  root   4096 Apr 10 08:01 tmp
-rw-r--r--  1 admin admin   256 Apr 10 08:00 notes.txt

title: Systemd Service Dependency Grapher
prompt: Write a Python script `service_deps.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing systemd-like service definitions, each with `name`, `requires` (array of service names), and `wanted_by` (array). Build a dependency graph and detect: (1) circular dependencies, (2) missing dependencies (required service not defined), (3) the correct startup order (topological sort, ignoring cycles). Write `service_order.txt` with the startup order (one service per line) and `service_issues.txt` listing any circular or missing dependency issues found.

title: Log-Based Request Rate Limiter Simulator
prompt: Write a Python script `rate_limit.py` that reads an access log (first argument) and a rate limit config (second argument, JSON with `requests_per_minute` and `ban_duration_minutes`). For each IP, count requests per minute window. If any IP exceeds the limit in any window, flag it. Write `rate_limit_report.txt` with: per-IP max requests/minute, flagged IPs with their peak rate and the minute window when the violation occurred, and total flagged IPs count.

title: Full Infrastructure Monitoring Dashboard Generator
prompt: Write a Python script `dashboard_gen.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) containing an array of server objects, each with `hostname`, `ip`, `cpu_percent`, `memory_percent`, `disk_percent`, `uptime_seconds`, `services` (array of objects with `name` and `status`). Generate: (1) `dashboard.html` — a self-contained HTML page with a table showing all servers color-coded (green < 70%, yellow 70-90%, red > 90% for resource metrics), service status, and uptime formatted as `Xd Xh Xm`. (2) `dashboard_data.json` — summary JSON with `total_servers`, `healthy_count` (all metrics < 90% and all services running), `critical_count`, `avg_cpu`, `avg_memory`, `avg_disk`. (3) `alerts.txt` — one line per critical condition found.

title: Incident Timeline Reconstructor
prompt: Write a Python script `incident_timeline.py` that reads multiple log files (all .log files listed as arguments) and a JSON incident definition file (last argument, has `start_time`, `end_time` in ISO format, and `keywords` array). The script must: (1) filter all log entries within the incident time window, (2) highlight entries matching any keyword, (3) build a chronological timeline merging all sources, (4) identify the first occurrence of each keyword across all logs. Write `timeline.txt` with format `[TIMESTAMP] [SOURCE] [MATCHED_KEYWORDS or -] LINE`. Write `incident_summary.json` with `total_events`, `matched_events`, `sources` (array), `keyword_first_seen` (object mapping keyword to first timestamp), `event_rate_per_minute`.

title: Automated Runbook Generator
prompt: Write a Python script `runbook_gen.py` that reads a JSON file (first argument) defining a service architecture with `services` (array of objects with `name`, `port`, `health_endpoint`, `depends_on`, `restart_cmd`, `log_path`) and `alert_rules` (array with `name`, `condition`, `service`, `severity`). Generate `runbook.md` — a Markdown runbook document with: (1) a service overview table, (2) a dependency diagram in text art showing the service graph, (3) for each alert rule, a troubleshooting section with step-by-step instructions including checking logs, verifying dependencies, and restart procedures. Also generate `quick_ref.txt` with a condensed one-line-per-service reference: `SERVICE PORT HEALTH_URL RESTART_CMD`.

input.architecture.json: {"services":[{"name":"nginx","port":80,"health_endpoint":"/health","depends_on":[],"restart_cmd":"systemctl restart nginx","log_path":"/var/log/nginx/error.log"},{"name":"webapp","port":3000,"health_endpoint":"/api/health","depends_on":["nginx","postgres","redis"],"restart_cmd":"systemctl restart webapp","log_path":"/var/log/webapp/app.log"},{"name":"postgres","port":5432,"health_endpoint":null,"depends_on":[],"restart_cmd":"systemctl restart postgresql","log_path":"/var/log/postgresql/postgresql-14-main.log"},{"name":"redis","port":6379,"health_endpoint":null,"depends_on":[],"restart_cmd":"systemctl restart redis","log_path":"/var/log/redis/redis-server.log"},{"name":"worker","port":null,"health_endpoint":null,"depends_on":["postgres","redis"],"restart_cmd":"systemctl restart worker","log_path":"/var/log/worker/worker.log"},{"name":"scheduler","port":null,"health_endpoint":null,"depends_on":["postgres"],"restart_cmd":"systemctl restart scheduler","log_path":"/var/log/scheduler/scheduler.log"}],"alert_rules":[{"name":"WebappDown","condition":"health check fails 3 times","service":"webapp","severity":"critical"},{"name":"HighCPU","condition":"CPU > 90% for 5 min","service":"webapp","severity":"warning"},{"name":"DBConnExhausted","condition":"active connections > 190","service":"postgres","severity":"critical"},{"name":"RedisMemory","condition":"memory usage > 80%","service":"redis","severity":"warning"},{"name":"WorkerBacklog","condition":"queue depth > 10000","service":"worker","severity":"warning"}]}

title: Capacity Planning Forecaster
prompt: Write a Python script `capacity_plan.py` that reads a CSV file (first argument) with columns `date,cpu_avg,memory_avg,disk_used_gb,request_count` containing 30 days of historical data. The script must: (1) compute daily growth rates for each metric, (2) use linear regression (from scratch, no numpy — use the least squares formula) to forecast when each metric will hit a critical threshold (CPU 90%, Memory 90%, Disk at capacity given in second argument as total GB), (3) determine which resource will hit capacity first. Write `capacity_forecast.json` with per-metric growth rate, projected critical date, and days remaining. Write `capacity_report.txt` with a human-readable summary including recommendations (add capacity, optimize, etc. based on urgency).

title: Infrastructure-as-Code Config Drift Detector
prompt: Write a Python script `drift_detect.py` that compares a desired state configuration (first argument, JSON) against an actual state (second argument, JSON). Both files describe servers with nested structures: `hostname`, `packages` (dict of name:version), `services` (dict of name:status), `firewall_rules` (array of rule objects), `users` (array of user objects with `name` and `groups`). The script must detect: added/removed/changed packages, service status mismatches, extra/missing firewall rules, user/group differences. Write `drift_report.json` with structured diff per server. Write `drift_summary.txt` with a human-readable report listing each server's drift items, categorized by type, with a total drift score (count of all differences) per server and overall.

title: Post-Mortem Report Generator from Logs
prompt: Write a Python script `postmortem.py` that reads an incident metadata JSON file (first argument) with `title`, `severity`, `start_time`, `end_time`, `responders` (array of names), `affected_services` (array), and multiple log files (remaining arguments). The script must: (1) extract all log entries within the incident window, (2) automatically detect the sequence of events by parsing timestamps and severity keywords, (3) identify the likely root cause by finding the earliest ERROR/FATAL entry, (4) compute time-to-detect (first ALERT after first ERROR) and time-to-resolve (end_time minus start_time). Generate `postmortem.md` with sections: Summary, Timeline (chronological events with source), Root Cause Analysis, Impact (duration and affected services), Resolution, and Action Items (auto-generated based on findings — e.g., if connection exhaustion is found, suggest connection pool tuning). Also generate `postmortem_data.json` with structured fields: `duration_minutes`, `root_cause_event`, `time_to_detect_seconds`, `total_events`, `events_by_severity`.
